scoring.clinicalImpactScore: Axis 1 — integer 1-5 stars: impact to the clinical discipline
scoring.littBusinessImpact: Axis 2 — integer 0-10: magnitude of effect on the LITT business
scoring.verified: verified | partial | unverified — provenance of the key figures

runs.note: Initial 24-month baseline. Dedicated neuro-oncology and epilepsy research agents were unreliable; neuro-oncology coverage drawn from the LITT-specific and adjacent-therapy scans, epilepsy coverage backfilled with targeted in-window searches.

Trailing-2-month delta, one day after the 24-month baseline. Five web-enabled domain agents ran in parallel (neuro-oncology, LITT-specific, epilepsy, regulatory/device, adjacent/imaging). LITT-specific and epilepsy domains returned NO verifiable in-window items (near-misses documented: BBB-permeability paper 2026-04-13 and RNS post-approval 2026-04-24 both fell just before the window). All deltas cluster at ASCO 2026 (May 29-Jun 2) plus a Jun 18 Novocure readout and discrete device/regulatory items. Concurrent prior intra-day passes created duplicate event records and an inconsistent run log; this run reconciles to a single deduplicated state (3 event-twins merged, keeping the richer record). Primary publisher/press pages were proxy-blocked (403); every in-window date was corroborated across multiple independent search sources, with single-source numerics flagged partial.

Curated landmark backfill extending the library to 2020 using verified references (LAANTERN registry, society guidelines/position statements, BBB-permeability mechanism, MTLE durability, REMASTer RCT, RN/combination evidence). Reliable citation-level provenance; not an exhaustive bibliography.

Trailing-2-month deltas. Clinical/peer-reviewed sweep found little genuinely new in-window (most 2026 LITT items - pembrolizumab RCT, BBB mechanism, LAANTERN MTLE - date Feb-Apr 2026, just before window, and are already in the corpus). Value was in ecosystem/regulatory deltas (GT Medical, NeuroOne, Medtronic leadership, uniQure/ClearPoint) and selection-imaging (Pixclara NDA, RAD101, zongertinib CNS data, INDIGO extended follow-up).

items.title: LITT + adjuvant pembrolizumab in recurrent high-grade astrocytoma (randomized Ph1/2b)
items.citation: LITT and adjuvant pembrolizumab in recurrent high-grade astrocytoma. Nat Commun. 2026. NCT02311582 (WashU).
items.studyDesign: Phase 1 dose-escalation + randomized Phase 2b (LITT+pembro vs non-LITT surgery+pembro)
items.results: Median OS 11.8 vs 5.2 mo; 18-mo survival 42% vs 0%; median PFS 4.5 vs 1.6 mo; IDH-wt OS 11.1 vs 4.8 mo; no DLTs
items.significance: Formal p-values UNVERIFIED; very small/imbalanced control arm
items.limitations: Tiny control arm (n=6); single-institution lead-in; not a definitive Phase 3
items.evidenceStrength: Randomized Ph2b, small; promising not definitive
items.clinicalBottomLine: First randomized signal that LITT-mediated BBB disruption can potentiate checkpoint blockade with a meaningful survival difference.
items.whyMatters: Flagship mechanism-of-action asset for LITT; Monteris publicized it.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — positions LITT as an immunotherapy enabler, not just cytoreduction
items.changeNote: Verified: Nat Commun 2026, NCT02311582; median OS 11.8 vs 5.2 mo (HR 0.17, P=0.0002) corroborated.

items.title: LITT vs open resective surgery for drug-resistant epilepsy — meta-analysis (3,873 patients)
items.citation: Comparative assessment of LITT and open resective surgery for drug-resistant epilepsy: meta-analysis of 3873 patients. J Neurosurg. 2025;144(1):35.
items.results: Seizure freedom 52.5% (LITT) vs 67.1% (open resection)
items.significance: Pooled, heterogeneous source studies
items.limitations: Non-randomized inputs; selection bias; efficacy gap favors open surgery
items.evidenceStrength: Large meta-analysis of mostly observational data
items.clinicalBottomLine: LITT trades modestly lower seizure-freedom for a markedly less invasive profile — the central honest trade-off in epilepsy ablation.
items.whyMatters: Double-edged: must be positioned on morbidity/recovery, not seizure-freedom superiority.
items.competitiveImpact: neutral — quantifies LITT's lower seizure-freedom vs open surgery; offset by morbidity advantage

items.title: LITT for high-grade glioma — systematic review, meta-analysis & meta-regression
items.citation: LITT for high-grade glioma: systematic review, meta-analysis and meta-regression. Neurosurg Focus. 2025;59(2):E10.
items.results: Concludes LITT effective with acceptable safety; pooled estimates UNVERIFIED
items.significance: Heterogeneous, largely non-comparative inputs
items.limitations: Low-tier primary data; calls for randomized validation
items.clinicalBottomLine: Best current synthesis-level evidence for LITT in HGG, tempered by source quality.
items.whyMatters: Supports utilization messaging for HGG with explicit evidence caveats.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — favorable pooled conclusion for LITT in HGG
items.changeNote: Venue/date confirmed (Neurosurg Focus 59(2):E10, Aug 2025); pooled figures not independently corroborated.

items.title: LITT for brain metastases — imaging guidelines for trials & practice (RANO-BM adapted)
items.citation: LITT for Brain Metastases: Imaging Guidelines for Clinical Trials and Practice. Neuro-Oncology. 2026. PMID 42218818.
items.results: Dual-baseline RANO-BM-adapted criteria; standardized post-LITT imaging; warns early (<3-4 mo) changes are unreliable
items.significance: Consensus, not outcomes data
items.limitations: Expert opinion; adoption pending
items.clinicalBottomLine: Standardizes how LITT-treated metastases are measured — infrastructure for credible comparative trials.
items.whyMatters: Enables rigorous LITT trials and defensible response assessment.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — builds trial/response-assessment infrastructure around LITT

items.title: LaSAR BeaM — randomized trial: LITT vs surgery + adjuvant reirradiation (recurrent brain mets)
items.citation: LaSAR BeaM. NCT07053033, MD Anderson (2025-0377).
items.studyDesign: Prospective comparative (randomized vs surgery) trial — recruiting
items.results: No data yet (just opened)
items.significance: n/a
items.limitations: No results yet
items.clinicalBottomLine: One of very few prospective comparative LITT trials in metastases — key Level-I pipeline item.
items.whyMatters: High strategic value for future comparative-effectiveness positioning.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens (if positive) — head-to-head vs surgery + reRT

items.title: Multicenter MR-guided LITT for brain metastases (Beijing Tiantan)
items.citation: Multicenter trial of MR-guided LITT for brain metastases. NCT07132203 (Beijing Tiantan).
items.results: Registry status only; no peer-reviewed outcomes yet
items.significance: n/a
items.limitations: No published outcomes located
items.clinicalBottomLine: A completed multicenter metastases LITT trial whose results are pending.
items.whyMatters: Watch for readout — potential to strengthen the metastasis evidence base.
items.competitiveImpact: neutral — pending readout

items.title: LITT vs craniotomy — comparative safety/effectiveness for deep-seated lesions (+ cost-effectiveness)
items.citation: LITTability cohort (PMC12130115, 2025) + LITT cost-effectiveness preprint (medRxiv 2025.10.26.25338812).
items.results: LITT EOR ~85.4% vs craniotomy ~77%; ~10% absolute reduction in major complications; complications minimized at <=15 cm3; incremental survival ~3.07 mo at ~$2,445/mo
items.significance: Non-randomized; cost item is a non-peer-reviewed preprint
items.limitations: Selection bias; preprint cost data
items.clinicalBottomLine: LITT offers a favorable safety profile for difficult-to-access lesions, plausibly cost-favorable.
items.whyMatters: Core minimally-invasive / deep-seated value proposition with access & cost angles.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — supports the deep-seated, minimally invasive case vs open surgery
items.changeNote: Could not corroborate a discrete 2025 'LITT vs craniotomy for deep-seated lesions' publication; nearest match is a 2024 Neurosurg Focus RN cost study. Treat as unverified pending a citable source.

items.title: LITT for IDH-wildtype recurrent GBM — Scandinavian two-center cohort
items.citation: LITT for IDH-wildtype recurrent GBM: a two-center cohort. Acta Neurochir. 2026.
items.results: Survival figures UNVERIFIED (full text blocked)
items.significance: Small, retrospective, no comparator
items.limitations: Underpowered
items.clinicalBottomLine: Adds independent European real-world experience in molecularly defined recurrent GBM.
items.whyMatters: Incremental real-world support for recurrent GBM utilization.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens (mildly) — independent cohort
items.changeNote: Verified: Acta Neurochir 2026 (Rigshospitalet n=16 + Karolinska n=14), 30 IDH-wt rGBM.

items.title: Hybrid open resection + LITT for newly diagnosed & recurrent GBM (staged)
items.citation: Hybrid therapy integrating open resection with LITT for GBM. PMID 40552902 (2025).
items.results: No intraoperative/ablation complications; stable exams; no new deficits
items.significance: Anecdotal
items.limitations: n~2; no survival/comparative data
items.clinicalBottomLine: Combining craniotomy and LITT for multifocal GBM is technically feasible.
items.whyMatters: Expands LITT use-cases; far too small to change practice.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens (mildly) — novel hybrid use-case
items.changeNote: Confirmed as Operative Neurosurgery 2026 (epub ~Jun 2025; PMID 40552902) - a 2-patient illustrative case report, not a series; venue differs from prior label.

items.title: Radiation necrosis after SRS — ISRS systematic review & recommendations (LITT vs bevacizumab/surgery)
items.citation: ISRS systematic review & recommendations on symptomatic brain radiation necrosis after SRS. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys.
items.venue: Int. J. Radiation Oncology Biology Physics (Red Journal)
items.results: High control with bevacizumab, surgery, or LITT — all from low-level, non-comparative evidence
items.significance: No head-to-head RCTs; base article may predate window (cite 2025 derivative discussion)
items.limitations: No universal RN grading; evidence-limited
items.clinicalBottomLine: LITT is endorsed as a reasonable RN option, without superiority evidence.
items.whyMatters: Radiation necrosis is one of LITT's clearest differentiated indications.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — society-level positioning for RN, evidence-limited
items.changeNote: Verified ISRS systematic review/recommendations on symptomatic RN after SRS; corrected to print 2024 (epub 2023), not 2025.

items.title: SLAH single-center learning curve & framework to manage failures
items.citation: Raghu et al. A single-center learning curve for stereotactic laser amygdalohippocampotomy and a surgical framework to manage failures. Epilepsia. 2025. PMID 39704262.
items.results: After failed SLAH: 46% seizure-free with repeat SLAH, 63% with ATL at 1 yr
items.significance: Single-center, retrospective
items.limitations: Learning-curve & selection effects
items.clinicalBottomLine: Operator experience and a structured failure pathway materially affect SLAH outcomes.
items.whyMatters: Training/technique is a lever on LITT epilepsy outcomes — actionable for proctoring & messaging.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — links training to outcomes; supports proctoring programs

items.title: Neuropsychological outcomes: hippocampus-sparing ATL vs SLAH in dominant-hemisphere TLE
items.citation: George et al. Neuropsychological outcomes of hippocampus-sparing anterior temporal lobectomy vs stereotactic laser amygdalohippocampectomy in language-dominant TLE. Epilepsia. 2025. PMID 40183790.
items.results: SLAH comparable seizure outcomes to HSATL; superior verbal memory retention; fewer neuropsych sequelae than standard ATL
items.significance: Non-randomized
items.limitations: Selection bias; single-center
items.clinicalBottomLine: SLAH preserves cognition (verbal memory) better than open resection at comparable seizure control in dominant-hemisphere TLE.
items.whyMatters: Cognitive sparing is a primary LITT selling point in dominant-hemisphere MTLE referrals.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — cognitive-preservation advantage vs open resection

items.title: LITT for mesial temporal lobe epilepsy in children
items.citation: LITT for the treatment of mesial temporal lobe epilepsy in children. 2025. PMID 39887192.
items.results: Safe/effective; more favorable seizure-freedom with hippocampal atrophy than nonlesional MTLE
items.significance: Retrospective
items.limitations: Single-cohort; selection
items.clinicalBottomLine: LITT is effective in pediatric MTLE, with imaging (hippocampal atrophy) predicting better outcomes.
items.whyMatters: Extends LITT into pediatric MTLE and supports imaging-based selection.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — pediatric MTLE evidence + selection criteria

items.title: MRI-guided laser ablation for pediatric drug-resistant epilepsy — systematic review & IPD meta-analysis
items.citation: MRI-guided laser ablation therapy for pediatric drug-resistant epilepsy: systematic review and IPD meta-analysis. Neurology (AAN 2024) P3-8.006.
items.results: Supports MRgLITT as a minimally invasive alternative to open resection in pediatric DRE
items.significance: Abstract-level; observational inputs
items.limitations: Conference abstract; heterogeneity
items.clinicalBottomLine: Pooled pediatric data support LITT as a minimally invasive option across DRE etiologies.
items.whyMatters: Broadens pediatric epilepsy evidence across focal/HH/MTLE.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — pediatric pooled evidence

items.title: MRI-guided LITT in epilepsy — indications, technique, outcome (adult single-center)
items.citation: MRI-guided LITT in epilepsy: indications, technique and outcome in an adult population. Acta Neurochir. 2025.
items.population: Adult drug-resistant epilepsy (mixed etiologies incl. HH, FCD)
items.results: Supports LITT efficacy in well-localized lesions; QoL gains in seizure worry & social functioning
items.significance: Single-center, retrospective
items.limitations: Small; mixed pathology
items.clinicalBottomLine: Real-world adult epilepsy series reinforcing LITT for well-localized targets incl. hypothalamic hamartoma.
items.whyMatters: Adds HH and focal coverage with QoL signal.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens (mildly) — real-world adult series

items.title: Reoperation after failed laser ablation for epilepsy in pediatric patients (multicenter)
items.citation: Outcomes of reoperation following failed laser ablation surgery for epilepsy in pediatric patients. J Neurosurg Pediatr. 2025;35(4):369.
items.results: 5/10 seizure-free after a second LITT
items.significance: Small subset; retrospective
items.limitations: Small numbers
items.clinicalBottomLine: Repeat LITT can salvage seizure control after an initial failure.
items.whyMatters: Supports durability/repeatability of laser ablation — addresses a common physician objection.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — repeatability counters durability objections

items.title: Vorasidenib (Voranigo) FDA approval for IDH-mutant grade 2 glioma (INDIGO)
items.citation: FDA approval of vorasidenib; INDIGO phase 3 (Mellinghoff et al., NEJM 2023); FDA Approval Summary, Clin Cancer Res. 2025;31(21):4412.
items.results: PFS 27.7 vs 11.1 mo; HR 0.39 (95% CI 0.27-0.56; p<0.001); TTNI HR ~0.26. ASCO 2026 extended follow-up: median PFS now 44.1 mo; only 23.8% required next intervention; cognition/QoL stable.
items.significance: Highly significant (p<0.001)
items.limitations: Grade 2 only; surrogate endpoints; OS immature
items.clinicalBottomLine: An oral targeted therapy that defers radiation/chemo in low-grade IDH-mutant glioma — practice-changing.
items.whyMatters: Shifts grade 2 IDH-mutant management toward drug-first, but raises the value of early molecular tissue (biopsy ± LITT cytoreduction).
items.competitiveImpact: weakens (mildly) — drug-first sequencing can defer ablation in grade 2 IDH-mutant glioma
items.changeNote: Verified: FDA approval 2024-08-06; INDIGO trial NEJM 2023 (mPFS 27.7 vs 11.1 mo).

items.title: METIS phase 3 — TTFields after SRS for NSCLC brain metastases
items.citation: METIS trial (Novocure), final results; ASTRO 2025; Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys.
items.venue: Int. J. Radiation Oncology Biology Physics (Red Journal)
items.population: 1-10 newly diagnosed NSCLC brain metastases post-SRS
items.results: Met primary endpoint; intracranial progression 46.9% vs 59.4% (12 mo), 53.6% vs 65.2% (24 mo); no neurocognitive/QoL decline
items.significance: Met primary endpoint (not OS)
items.limitations: Composite endpoint; device adherence; NSCLC-specific
items.clinicalBottomLine: TTFields is now an evidence-backed non-invasive maintenance adjunct after SRS for NSCLC brain mets.
items.whyMatters: Competes for the same post-SRS patients LITT might otherwise see; positions LITT later (salvage).
items.competitiveImpact: weakens — strengthens a non-invasive post-SRS pathway competing with LITT

items.title: SRS for brain metastases — expanding indications (authoritative synthesis)
items.citation: Mansouri, Ozair, Bhanja, et al. Nat Rev Clin Oncol. 2025;22(5):327-347.
items.results: Endorses fractionated & preoperative SRS to reduce radiation necrosis & LMD; supports SRS in polymetastatic disease and with checkpoint/targeted therapy
items.significance: Narrative review; partly ahead of level-1 evidence
items.limitations: Expert opinion
items.clinicalBottomLine: SRS indications are broadening, with explicit strategies to reduce radiation necrosis.
items.whyMatters: Lower-necrosis SRS and broader SRS use erode two LITT strongholds (RN and deep/small lesions).
items.competitiveImpact: weakens — fewer lesions fall through to surgery/LITT; lower-necrosis SRS narrows the RN niche

items.title: Preoperative vs postoperative SRS — NRG-BN012 (accruing) + phase 1 data
items.citation: NRG-BN012 (phase 3, accruing); supporting phase 1, Adv Radiat Oncol. 2025.
items.results: Phase 1: preop SRS reduced nodular LMD (7.4% vs 27.1%, p=0.002)
items.significance: Phase 1 small; phase 3 pending
items.limitations: Status change, not readout
items.clinicalBottomLine: Preoperative SRS is gaining traction as a lower-toxicity sequencing strategy.
items.whyMatters: If preop SRS lowers radiation necrosis, it erodes a downstream LITT indication — trial to watch.
items.competitiveImpact: weakens (potential) — could reduce future radiation-necrosis volume

items.title: Joint EANM/EANO/RANO/SNMMI PET imaging of brain metastases — practice guideline v1.0
items.citation: Joint EANM/EANO/RANO/SNMMI practice guideline. Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging. 2025.
items.venue: Eur. J. Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging
items.results: Positions amino-acid/FDG PET to resolve recurrence-vs-radiation-necrosis where MRI cannot
items.significance: Consensus, not outcomes data
items.limitations: Tracer availability varies
items.clinicalBottomLine: PET is now formally positioned to adjudicate post-radiation ambiguity in brain mets.
items.whyMatters: Double-edged: better non-invasive discrimination may reduce diagnostic LITT, but sharpens selection of true recurrence where LITT fits.
items.competitiveImpact: neutral — changes imaging-driven selection in both directions

items.title: Intracerebroventricular bivalent (EGFR + IL-13Rα2) CAR-T in recurrent GBM — phase 1
items.citation: O'Rourke lab (Penn). Nat Med. 2025; presented ASCO 2025.
items.results: Tumor growth slowed in ~62%; acceptable intraventricular safety (numbers approximate, full text blocked)
items.significance: Phase 1, small, short follow-up; responses transient
items.limitations: No survival endpoint
items.clinicalBottomLine: Promising but early; not yet practice-changing.
items.whyMatters: Future systemic competitor / potential combination partner for the recurrent-GBM space LITT serves.
items.competitiveImpact: weakens (long-horizon) — maturing recurrent-GBM systemic pipeline; possible future combo
items.changeNote: Verified: Nat Med 2025;31:2778-2787; ICV bivalent EGFR+IL-13Ra2 CAR-T phase 1, rGBM.

items.title: TTFields + temozolomide + pembrolizumab in newly diagnosed GBM (2-THE-TOP; EF-41/KEYNOTE-D58 phase 3)
items.citation: 2-THE-TOP phase 2; EF-41/KEYNOTE-D58 phase 3 (ongoing).
items.results: Phase 2: mPFS 12 vs 5.8 mo; mOS 24.8 vs 14.6 mo (historical comparator — interpret cautiously)
items.significance: Non-randomized comparator; awaiting phase 3
items.limitations: Small, non-randomized
items.clinicalBottomLine: Reinforces TTFields as the entrenched non-invasive adjunct in upfront GBM.
items.whyMatters: TTFields increasingly owns the device-adjunct narrative in newly diagnosed GBM.
items.competitiveImpact: weakens (share-of-voice) — competing non-invasive adjunct in upfront GBM
items.changeNote: Verified: 2-THE-TOP (NCT03405792), Med (Cell Press) Jun 2025; PFS 12 vs 5.8 mo.

items.title: Medtronic Visualase V2 MRI-guided laser ablation system — FDA 510(k) clearance
items.citation: Medtronic press release; corroborated by MassDevice, MD+DI.
items.results: Next-gen LITT platform: redesigned software, capital upgrades; emphasizes submillimetric resolution + automated phase-drift correction (thermometry). Same indications (epilepsy, tumors, RN).
items.significance: Regulatory/marketing claim, not clinical outcome
items.limitations: No new clinical data
items.clinicalBottomLine: The primary LITT competitor refreshed its platform with a thermometry-accuracy differentiation story.
items.whyMatters: Resets Medtronic's selling cycle; raises the thermometry/automation bar NeuroBlate must answer.
items.competitiveImpact: weakens — direct #1 competitor platform refresh

items.title: ClearPoint Prism Neuro Laser Therapy System — 1.5T MRI compatibility (FDA 510(k))
items.citation: ClearPoint Neuro / CLS press release; corroborated by StockTitan, Cision.
items.results: Prism (1064nm) cleared for 1.5T MRI guidance (previously 3.0T-only); company cites ~60% US / >70% global 1.5T installed base — roughly doubling addressable market
items.significance: Regulatory claim
items.limitations: Adoption pending
items.clinicalBottomLine: A third LITT entrant just unlocked the majority of MRI suites it was previously boxed out of.
items.whyMatters: Makes Prism a credible NeuroBlate alternative at any 1.5T site — meaningfully expands competition.
items.competitiveImpact: weakens — doubles a competitor's addressable installed base

items.title: Monteris Medical — $28M Series E growth equity
items.citation: Monteris Medical press release; corroborated by PitchBook.
items.results: $28M Series E (InnovaHealth, Birchview; new: OSF Ventures, CU Healthcare Innovation Fund) to accelerate revenue & innovation. Not an acquisition.
items.significance: Exact date UNVERIFIED (proxy blocked)
items.limitations: Date approximate
items.clinicalBottomLine: Monteris raised growth capital to scale NeuroBlate revenue and innovation.
items.whyMatters: Own-company capital context against scaled rivals.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — internal capital to fund roadmap
items.changeNote: Verified: $28M Series E + $35M Trinity credit facility, announced 2026-03-12.

items.title: Medtronic Stealth AXiS — FDA clearance for cranial & ENT (robotics + navigation)
items.citation: Medtronic press release; corroborated by Medical Device Network, MassDevice.
items.results: Integrated planning + navigation + robotics cleared for cranial/ENT (built on Mazor + StealthStation) EU CE Mark obtained 2026-04-28 (spine + cranial).
items.significance: Regulatory
items.limitations: No clinical outcomes
items.clinicalBottomLine: Medtronic can now offer a bundled robot + navigation + LITT cranial suite from a single vendor.
items.whyMatters: Ecosystem bundling Monteris (single-product specialist) cannot match alone.
items.competitiveImpact: weakens — enables bundled cranial robotics+LITT offering
items.changeNote: In-window CE Mark (EU), 2026-04-28: Stealth AXiS authorized for spine + cranial in Europe — geographic expansion of the navigation+robotics platform, distinct from the 2026-03-27 US cranial/ENT clearance. Verified via Medtronic newsroom URL slug + MassDevice / Ortho Spine News.

items.title: ClearPoint cranial ecosystem buildout — MR drill, Navigation v3.0, robotic prototype
items.citation: ClearPoint Neuro press releases (Velocity Alpha drill 510(k); Navigation Software v3.0; robotic neuro-navigation prototype, CNS 2025).
items.results: Velocity Alpha MR high-speed drill cleared (Apr 2026); Navigation Software v3.0 (cleared late 2024, full release 2H 2025); KUKA-based robotic neuro-navigation prototype shown Oct 2025 (not yet FDA-submitted)
items.significance: Mix of cleared + prototype
items.limitations: Robotics not yet submitted
items.clinicalBottomLine: ClearPoint is assembling an all-in-one cranial procedural ecosystem around its Prism LITT system.
items.whyMatters: Medium-term competitive threat — a LITT rival adding drill, navigation, and (future) robotics.
items.competitiveImpact: weakens — deepens a LITT competitor's cranial ecosystem
items.changeNote: Verified: Velocity Alpha MR drill first clinical use 2026-04-22 plus robotic neuro-navigation prototype + ClearPoint 3.0.

items.title: Cortechs.ai NeuroQuant Brain Tumor — FDA-cleared AI segmentation expanded to mets & meningiomas
items.citation: Cortechs.ai press release; Applied Radiology coverage.
items.results: Adds automated volumetrics for brain metastases and meningiomas; imports into RT/neurosurgical planning
items.significance: Vendor data; outcome benefit unproven
items.limitations: Reader-assist, not autonomous; no RCT
items.clinicalBottomLine: Volumetric AI longitudinal tracking is becoming routine across tumor types.
items.whyMatters: Better selection + objective post-LITT response surveillance; favors earlier, smaller-lesion intervention.
items.competitiveImpact: neutral-to-strengthens — aids candidate ID and post-ablation surveillance

items.title: LITT reimbursement landscape — commercial policies evolving; no national coverage decision
items.citation: Multiple payer policies (Premera, Cigna, Blue Cross NC, etc.); CPT 61736/61737; Medtronic 2025 coding guide.
items.results: No national NCD and generally no Medicare LCD specific to LITT. Commercial coverage increasingly favorable for epilepsy and tumors/RN, but some payers still label certain neurological LITT indications investigational. CPT 61736/61737 remain the intracranial codes.
items.significance: Landscape, not a single decision
items.limitations: Fragmented; some 'investigational' labels persist
items.clinicalBottomLine: Coverage fragmentation and absence of national clarity remain a category-wide access barrier.
items.whyMatters: Shared headwind affecting all LITT vendors; AAN/AES guidelines do not yet address LITT.
items.competitiveImpact: neutral — category-wide access barrier, not LITT-vendor-specific

items.title: Sequential robot-assisted stereotactic biopsy + LITT workflow (technique)
items.citation: How I do it: sequential robot-assisted stereotactic biopsy and LITT. Acta Neurochir. 2025.
items.results: Limiting to two biopsy specimens reduces air/hemorrhage that degrade MR thermometry, preserving ablation accuracy
items.significance: Technical note, not comparative outcomes
items.limitations: Lower-tier; no outcomes
items.clinicalBottomLine: Same-session robotic biopsy + LITT is operationally maturing.
items.whyMatters: Supports the 'diagnose-and-ablate' value proposition and robotics/iMRI integration.
items.competitiveImpact: strengthens — reinforces diagnose-and-ablate + robotics integration

items.title: NEO-TACTICS phase 2 — neoadjuvant fractionated SRT then resection for brain metastases
items.citation: Mitsuya K, et al. NEO-TACTICS: neoadjuvant fractionated stereotactic radiotherapy followed by resection for brain metastases (multicenter single-arm phase II). J Clin Oncol 44, 2026 (suppl 16), abstr 2003; ASCO 2026.
items.population: Resectable brain metastasis 2-5 cm (one index lesion +/- <=3 non-index <2 cm)
items.endpoints: Local recurrence; intracranial PFS; leptomeningeal dissemination; radiation necrosis; cognition
items.results: Local recurrence 4.3% (6 mo), 16.4% (12 mo); intracranial PFS 72.5% (6 mo), 64.4% (12 mo); zero leptomeningeal dissemination; low radiation-necrosis rate; favorable cognitive preservation.
items.significance: Single-arm, no randomized comparator; hypothesis-supporting
items.limitations: Single-arm phase II, N=55, no control; requires phase 3 confirmation
items.clinicalBottomLine: Pre-op fractionated SRS before resection gave excellent local control with essentially no leptomeningeal spread and low necrosis — reinforcing preoperative SRS for resectable mets.
items.whyMatters: Consolidates the surgery+SRS paradigm and, by minimizing radiation necrosis, may shrink a downstream LITT (RN ablation) indication.
items.competitiveImpact: weakens (mildly) — advances preop-SRS, lowering future radiation-necrosis volume

items.title: NeoVax personalized neoantigen vaccine + pembrolizumab in newly diagnosed GBM (phase 1)
items.citation: Reardon DA, Wu CJ, et al. A personalized neoantigen vaccine to reprogram the immune landscape of glioblastoma (NeoVax + pembrolizumab). J Clin Oncol 44, 2026 (suppl 16), abstr 2006; ASCO 2026 (Dana-Farber).
items.studyDesign: Single-arm phase 1 (4 cohorts by MGMT status and pembrolizumab timing) vs propensity-matched historical controls
items.endpoints: Feasibility/safety; immunogenicity (neoantigen-specific T-cell response, intratumoral trafficking); exploratory OS vs matched controls
items.results: Neoantigen-specific T cells with intracranial trafficking, persisting >1 yr in some; median OS 36.9 mo (MGMT-methylated) and 19.0 mo (unmethylated) vs matched historical 25.3 and 16.7 mo; pembrolizumab before vaccine priming linked to longer OS.
items.significance: Encouraging but hypothesis-generating; not randomized
items.limitations: Phase 1, N=39, historical/propensity controls, small MGMT subgroups, selection bias (fit, dex-avoidant patients)
items.clinicalBottomLine: Personalized neoantigen vaccine + pembrolizumab is feasible, immunogenic, and shows a provocative survival signal in newly diagnosed GBM — promising but not practice-changing without randomized confirmation.
items.whyMatters: Immunotherapy momentum in GBM; LITT-induced transient BBB disruption is being explored to enhance such therapies — a potential future synergy rather than a competitor.
items.competitiveImpact: neutral — systemic GBM immunotherapy; possible future LITT combination partner
