items.title: Results of the NeuroBlate System first-in-humans Phase I clinical trial for recurrent glioblastoma
items.citation: Sloan AE, Ahluwalia MS, Valerio-Pascua J, et al. Results of the NeuroBlate System first-in-humans Phase I clinical trial for recurrent glioblastoma. J Neurosurg. 2013;118(6):1202-1219.
items.studyDesign: Prospective single-arm Phase I thermal dose-escalation safety/efficacy trial (first-in-human)
items.population: Adults with suspected supratentorial recurrent glioblastoma, 15-40 mm dimension, KPS >=60
items.endpoints: Safety/tolerability, thermal dose escalation, extent of tumor coverage, survival
items.results: Established feasibility/safety of gas-cooled side-firing NeuroBlate probe for deep hemispheric rGBM. Mean percentage of tumor treated at intent-to-treat dose was 78% ± 12% (range 57%-90%). Procedure-related morbidity informed subsequent dosing.
items.significance: Foundational first-in-human trial that brought the NeuroBlate (Monteris) MR-guided LITT platform to clinical use; basis for FDA clearance and all later NeuroBlate tumor series.
items.limitations: Very small Phase I cohort; no comparator; heterogeneous prior therapy; short follow-up; thermal dose still being optimized.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Landmark first-in-human NeuroBlate trial demonstrating LITT is technically feasible and tolerable for deep recurrent GBM, anchoring the modern LITT-for-tumor evidence base.
items.evidenceStrength: Prospective single-arm Phase I thermal dose-escalation safety/efficacy trial (first-in-human)
items.whyMatters: Landmark first-in-human NeuroBlate trial demonstrating LITT is technically feasible and tolerable for deep recurrent GBM, anchoring the modern LITT-for-tumor evidence base.

items.title: Magnetic Resonance Imaging-Guided Focused Laser Interstitial Thermal Therapy for Intracranial Lesions: Single-Institution Series
items.citation: Hawasli AH, Bagade S, Shimony JS, Miller-Thomas M, Leuthardt EC. Magnetic resonance imaging-guided focused laser interstitial thermal therapy for intracranial lesions: single-institution series. Neurosurgery. 2013;73(6):1007-1017.
items.population: Deep-seated/eloquent intracranial lesions (high-grade gliomas, metastases, others); lesions in frontal/parietal white matter and insula
items.endpoints: Perioperative complications, neurological outcome, recurrence-free/overall survival
items.results: Lesions 5.2-9.9 cm3. Transient deficits (aphasia, hemiparesis) in a minority; occasional focal seizures. Recurrence-free survival for recurrent HGG ~8.4 months; median PFS/OS for metastatic subgroup ~5.8 months. Low rate of permanent deficit.
items.significance: Early real-world NeuroBlate single-institution experience (Washington University/Leuthardt group) characterizing the learning curve and complication profile for deep/eloquent lesions.
items.limitations: Small mixed-pathology cohort; retrospective; no comparator; short follow-up; single surgeon/center experience.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Early-adopter series showing LITT can access deep/eloquent intracranial lesions with a low rate of permanent neurological injury, supporting its minimally invasive niche.
items.whyMatters: Early-adopter series showing LITT can access deep/eloquent intracranial lesions with a low rate of permanent neurological injury, supporting its minimally invasive niche.

items.title: The role of laser interstitial thermal therapy in enhancing progression-free survival of difficult-to-access high-grade gliomas: a multicenter study
items.citation: Mohammadi AM, Hawasli AH, Rodriguez A, et al. The role of laser interstitial thermal therapy in enhancing progression-free survival of difficult-to-access high-grade gliomas: a multicenter study. Cancer Med. 2014;3(4):971-979.
items.studyDesign: Multicenter retrospective cohort (Cleveland Clinic, Washington University, Wake Forest)
items.population: Difficult-to-access high-grade gliomas (24 GBM, 10 anaplastic), treated May 2011-Dec 2012
items.endpoints: Progression-free survival; relationship of extent of ablation (thermal-damage-threshold/TDT coverage) to PFS
items.results: Defined the TDT-line framework (yellow line = 43°C x 2 min; blue line = 43°C x 10 min) for quantifying ablation coverage. 1-year OS estimate ~68% ± 9%. Greater coverage of tumor volume by TDT lines was associated with improved progression-free survival.
items.significance: First multicenter dataset linking extent-of-ablation (TDT coverage) to outcome in HGG; established the volumetric ablation-quality concept that underpins later extent-of-ablation literature.
items.limitations: Retrospective; modest sample; heterogeneous histology and adjuvant therapy; no randomized comparator.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Key early extent-of-ablation paper: maximizing TDT-line coverage of difficult-to-access HGG correlates with better PFS, making ablation completeness a quality metric.
items.evidenceStrength: Multicenter retrospective cohort (Cleveland Clinic, Washington University, Wake Forest)
items.whyMatters: Key early extent-of-ablation paper: maximizing TDT-line coverage of difficult-to-access HGG correlates with better PFS, making ablation completeness a quality metric.

items.title: Hyperthermic Laser Ablation of Recurrent Glioblastoma Leads to Temporary Disruption of the Peritumoral Blood Brain Barrier
items.citation: Leuthardt EC, Duan C, Kim MJ, et al. Hyperthermic laser ablation of recurrent glioblastoma leads to temporary disruption of the peritumoral blood brain barrier. PLoS One. 2016;11(2):e0148613.
items.studyDesign: Prospective pilot mechanistic study with serial dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) MRI; LITT + doxorubicin
items.endpoints: Degree and time course of peritumoral BBB disruption (DCE-MRI permeability); safety of LITT + weekly doxorubicin during the BBB-open window
items.results: LITT produced measurable, transient peritumoral BBB disruption peaking in the early post-procedure period and resolving over several weeks, defining a therapeutic window for delivering otherwise BBB-impermeant agents (doxorubicin 20 mg/m2 IV weekly x6).
items.significance: Pioneering human evidence for the LITT-induced BBB-disruption mechanism, foundational rationale for LITT-plus-chemo/immuno combination strategies; predates the already-catalogued 2020/2025 BBB-permeability papers.
items.limitations: Small pilot; imaging surrogate for permeability; no efficacy endpoint powered; heterogeneous prior therapy.
items.clinicalBottomLine: First-in-human demonstration that LITT transiently opens the peritumoral BBB, providing the mechanistic basis for timed adjuvant drug delivery after ablation.
items.evidenceStrength: Prospective pilot mechanistic study with serial dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) MRI; LITT + doxorubicin
items.whyMatters: First-in-human demonstration that LITT transiently opens the peritumoral BBB, providing the mechanistic basis for timed adjuvant drug delivery after ablation.

items.title: Stereotactic laser ablation as treatment for brain metastases that recur after stereotactic radiosurgery: a multiinstitutional experience
items.citation: Ali MA, Carroll KT, Rennert RC, et al. Stereotactic laser ablation as treatment for brain metastases that recur after stereotactic radiosurgery: a multiinstitutional experience. Neurosurg Focus. 2016;41(4):E11.
items.population: Brain metastases recurring after prior SRS
items.endpoints: Local control / freedom from progression; relationship of ablation extent and adjuvant SRS to outcome
items.results: 9/26 lesions (35%) progressed; ALL progressions occurred in lesions with <80% ablation, while no progression occurred in lesions with >=80% ablation. 5 lesions treated with SLA + adjuvant hypofractionated SRS had no progression despite <80% ablation, suggesting consolidation SRS augments efficacy.
items.significance: Established the practice-defining >=80% ablation threshold for durable local control of post-SRS recurrent metastases and introduced the SLA-plus-consolidation-SRS paradigm.
items.limitations: Small retrospective cohort; short median follow-up (141 days); mixed primary histologies; selection bias.
items.clinicalBottomLine: For metastases recurring after SRS, achieving >=80% ablation predicts local control; consolidation SRS can rescue incomplete ablations - a core salvage-workflow principle.
items.whyMatters: For metastases recurring after SRS, achieving >=80% ablation predicts local control; consolidation SRS can rescue incomplete ablations - a core salvage-workflow principle.

items.title: Laser ablation after stereotactic radiosurgery: a multicenter prospective study in patients with metastatic brain tumors and radiation necrosis
items.citation: Ahluwalia M, Barnett GH, Deng D, et al. Laser ablation after stereotactic radiosurgery: a multicenter prospective study in patients with metastatic brain tumors and radiation necrosis. J Neurosurg. 2018;130(3):804-811.
items.studyDesign: Multicenter prospective study with pre-ablation stereotactic biopsy
items.population: Brain metastases with radiographic progression after SRS (biopsy: 20 recurrent tumor, 19 radiation necrosis, 3 nondiagnostic)
items.endpoints: Diagnostic yield of biopsy, ablation completeness, local control/complete response, progression-free survival
items.results: Biopsy distinguished recurrent tumor from radiation necrosis prior to LITT (diagnose-and-ablate workflow). Among completely ablated lesions, complete-response rates were 100% (radiation necrosis, 4 lesions) and 75% (tumor, 4 lesions). LITT controlled progressive enhancing lesions of both etiologies.
items.significance: Key prospective evidence validating the biopsy-then-ablate LITT workflow for post-SRS progressive lesions and supporting LITT for both radiation necrosis and recurrent tumor; the precursor study to the LAANTERN registry concept.
items.limitations: Modest sample; limited ablation data per subgroup; small numbers with complete ablation; variable follow-up.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Prospective backing for biopsy-guided LITT in post-SRS progression: confirm the diagnosis, then ablate - complete ablation yields high control for both radiation necrosis and recurrent metastasis.
items.evidenceStrength: Multicenter prospective study with pre-ablation stereotactic biopsy
items.whyMatters: Prospective backing for biopsy-guided LITT in post-SRS progression: confirm the diagnosis, then ablate - complete ablation yields high control for both radiation necrosis and recurrent metastasis.

items.title: Laser-interstitial thermal therapy compared to craniotomy for treatment of radiation necrosis or recurrent tumor in brain metastases failing radiosurgery
items.citation: Hong CS, Deng D, Vera A, Chiang VL. Laser-interstitial thermal therapy compared to craniotomy for treatment of radiation necrosis or recurrent tumor in brain metastases failing radiosurgery. J Neurooncol. 2019;142(2):309-317.
items.studyDesign: Single-institution retrospective comparative cohort (LITT vs craniotomy)
items.population: Brain metastases failing SRS: 42 recurrent tumor, 33 radiation necrosis; LITT (n=34) vs craniotomy (n=41)
items.endpoints: Local progression-free survival, overall survival, perioperative complications, steroid cessation, deficit improvement
items.results: Local control and OS similar between groups. PFS: 1-yr 72.2% (craniotomy) vs 61.1% (LITT), 2-yr 60.0% vs 61.1% (p=0.72). OS: 1-yr 69.0% vs 69.3%, 2-yr 56.6% vs 49.5% (p=0.90). Similar complication rates (24.4% vs 35.3%, p=0.32) and steroid cessation; craniotomy gave higher rates of pre-op deficit improvement.
items.significance: First direct LITT-vs-craniotomy comparison for post-SRS regrowing lesions, showing comparable oncologic outcomes - a cornerstone comparative-effectiveness reference for metastases (distinct from later deep-lesion comparisons).
items.limitations: Retrospective, single-center, non-randomized; selection bias (craniotomy for larger/symptomatic lesions); modest subgroup sizes.
items.clinicalBottomLine: For brain metastases failing SRS, LITT yields local control and survival comparable to craniotomy, though open surgery relieves mass-effect deficits faster - supporting LITT for deep/poor-surgical-candidate lesions.
items.evidenceStrength: Single-institution retrospective comparative cohort (LITT vs craniotomy)
items.whyMatters: For brain metastases failing SRS, LITT yields local control and survival comparable to craniotomy, though open surgery relieves mass-effect deficits faster - supporting LITT for deep/poor-surgical-candidate lesions.

items.title: Upfront Magnetic Resonance Imaging-Guided Stereotactic Laser-Ablation in Newly Diagnosed Glioblastoma: A Multicenter Review of Survival Outcomes Compared to a Matched Cohort of Biopsy-Only Patients
items.citation: Mohammadi AM, Sharma M, Beaumont TL, et al. Upfront magnetic resonance imaging-guided stereotactic laser-ablation in newly diagnosed glioblastoma: a multicenter review of survival outcomes compared to a matched cohort of biopsy-only patients. Neurosurgery. 2019;85(6):762-772.
items.studyDesign: Multicenter retrospective matched-cohort (upfront SLA vs biopsy-only); Cleveland Clinic, Washington University, Yale
items.population: Newly diagnosed deep-seated/unresectable glioblastoma treated upfront with LITT then chemoradiation, vs matched biopsy-only patients (matched on age, sex, location, volume)
items.sampleSize: 24 SLA patients + 24 matched biopsy-only controls (48 total)
items.endpoints: Progression-free survival, overall survival, disease-specific PFS/OS; prognostic stratification by extent of ablation (TDT-line coverage)
items.results: Identified three prognostic groups by extent of tumor ablation. Greater TDT-line coverage was associated with improved disease-specific PFS and OS; well-ablated patients showed survival benefit versus biopsy-only controls. (Exact median survival figures UNVERIFIED.)
items.significance: Best dedicated comparative dataset for UPFRONT LITT in newly diagnosed deep GBM, reinforcing extent-of-ablation as a survival driver and positioning LITT as cytoreduction for unresectable disease before chemoradiation.
items.limitations: Retrospective; small matched cohort; potential residual confounding; molecular markers (MGMT/IDH) incompletely characterized; no randomization.
items.clinicalBottomLine: For newly diagnosed unresectable/deep GBM, upfront LITT with maximal TDT-line ablation followed by chemoradiation is associated with better survival than biopsy alone - the key upfront-LITT comparative reference.
items.evidenceStrength: Multicenter retrospective matched-cohort (upfront SLA vs biopsy-only); Cleveland Clinic, Washington University, Yale
items.whyMatters: For newly diagnosed unresectable/deep GBM, upfront LITT with maximal TDT-line ablation followed by chemoradiation is associated with better survival than biopsy alone - the key upfront-LITT comparative reference.
items.changeNote: Verified: Mohammadi et al., Neurosurgery 2019;85(6):762-772 (PMID 30476325). Median OS figures exist in full text (proxy-blocked) but design/cohort confirmed.

items.title: Magnetic resonance-guided stereotactic laser ablation therapy for the treatment of pediatric brain tumors: a multiinstitutional retrospective study
items.citation: Arocho-Quinones EV, Lew SM, Handler MH, et al. Magnetic resonance-guided stereotactic laser ablation therapy for the treatment of pediatric brain tumors: a multiinstitutional retrospective study. J Neurosurg Pediatr. 2020;26(1):13-21.
items.studyDesign: Multi-institutional (17 North American centers) retrospective cohort
items.population: Pediatric brain tumors treated with stereotactic laser ablation 2008-2016 (76 low-grade WHO I-II, 10 high-grade III-IV); lobar 38.4%, deep 45.3%, cerebellar 16.3%
items.endpoints: Tumor volume change, safety/complications, factors associated with response
items.results: At last follow-up (mean 24 mo, median 18 mo), SLA-treated tumor volume decreased in 80.6% of patients with follow-up data. High-grade tumors were more likely than low-grade to be unchanged or larger after SLA. Established feasibility and safety profile in children.
items.significance: Largest multicenter pediatric tumor LITT dataset; defines the modality's role and limits (durable control mainly for low-grade lesions) in the under-represented pediatric population.
items.limitations: Retrospective; heterogeneous tumor types/locations; variable follow-up; small high-grade subgroup; no comparator.
items.clinicalBottomLine: LITT is a feasible minimally invasive option for pediatric brain tumors, achieving volume reduction in most low-grade lesions while high-grade tumors respond less reliably.
items.evidenceStrength: Multi-institutional (17 North American centers) retrospective cohort
items.whyMatters: LITT is a feasible minimally invasive option for pediatric brain tumors, achieving volume reduction in most low-grade lesions while high-grade tumors respond less reliably.

items.title: Laser interstitial thermal therapy for focal cerebral radiation necrosis: a case report and literature review
items.citation: Rahmathulla G, Recinos PF, Valerio JE, Chao S, Barnett GH. Laser interstitial thermal therapy for focal cerebral radiation necrosis: a case report and literature review. Stereotact Funct Neurosurg. 2012;90(3):192-200.
items.studyDesign: Case report with structured literature review
items.population: 74-year-old male with biopsy-proven cerebral radiation necrosis after SRS for NSCLC brain metastasis, refractory to high-dose steroids; presenting with dysphasia
items.endpoints: Symptom/steroid response, radiographic and clinical outcome after LITT
items.results: Described one of the earliest uses of LITT for steroid-refractory, biopsy-confirmed radiation necrosis, with clinical and imaging improvement, framing LITT as a salvage option when medical therapy fails.
items.significance: Origin reference for LITT in radiation-necrosis salvage - the conceptual seed for the now-major radiation-necrosis ablation literature (distinct from the already-catalogued 2023/2025 RN papers).
items.limitations: Single case; no comparator; limited follow-up; generalizability constrained by anecdotal design.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Earliest documented LITT for steroid-refractory radiation necrosis, establishing ablation as a salvage path when corticosteroids fail - historically foundational for the RN-salvage strategy.
items.evidenceStrength: Case report with structured literature review
items.whyMatters: Earliest documented LITT for steroid-refractory radiation necrosis, establishing ablation as a salvage path when corticosteroids fail - historically foundational for the RN-salvage strategy.

items.title: Real-time magnetic resonance-guided stereotactic laser amygdalohippocampotomy for mesial temporal lobe epilepsy
items.citation: Willie JT, Laxpati NG, Drane DL, et al. Neurosurgery. 2014 Jun;74(6):569-84.
items.studyDesign: Single-center prospective/retrospective case series with technical description (real-time MR thermometry-guided SLAH)
items.population: Adults with medically intractable mesial temporal lobe epilepsy (Emory series)
items.endpoints: Technical feasibility/safety of MR-guided SLAH; seizure freedom; complications
items.results: Established the SLAH technique using real-time MR thermal imaging; meaningful seizure freedom in MTS patients with favorable safety. Exact early-cohort seizure-freedom percentage UNVERIFIED.
items.significance: Foundational index paper establishing the SLAH technique and its safety/feasibility for MTLE.
items.limitations: Small sample, single center, limited follow-up, no randomized comparator.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Introduced MR-guided SLAH as a technically novel, safe, minimally invasive alternative to open temporal lobectomy for MTLE.
items.evidenceStrength: Single-center prospective/retrospective case series with technical description (real-time MR thermometry-guided SLAH)
items.whyMatters: Introduced MR-guided SLAH as a technically novel, safe, minimally invasive alternative to open temporal lobectomy for MTLE.
items.changeNote: Verified: Willie et al., Neurosurgery 2014;74(6):569-585 (PMID 24618797). ~54% Engel I in MTS subset.

items.title: Better object recognition and naming outcome with MRI-guided stereotactic laser amygdalohippocampotomy for temporal lobe epilepsy
items.citation: Drane DL, Loring DW, Voets NL, et al. Epilepsia. 2015 Jan;56(1):101-13.
items.studyDesign: Comparative neuropsychological cohort study (SLAH vs open temporal lobe resection)
items.population: Temporal lobe epilepsy patients undergoing SLAH vs open resection (dominant and non-dominant)
items.endpoints: Naming and recognition of common nouns and famous persons after surgery
items.results: Naming/recognition declines were significantly greater after open resection (32/39 declined) versus SLAH (0/17 declined), suggesting SLAH spares these functions.
items.significance: Landmark cognitive-outcome comparison demonstrating SLAH's naming/object-recognition-sparing advantage over open ATL.
items.limitations: Non-randomized, modest SLAH sample, single institution, potential selection bias.
items.clinicalBottomLine: SLAH markedly reduces naming/object-recognition decline versus open temporal resection, supporting its cognitive-sparing advantage.
items.evidenceStrength: Comparative neuropsychological cohort study (SLAH vs open temporal lobe resection)
items.whyMatters: SLAH markedly reduces naming/object-recognition decline versus open temporal resection, supporting its cognitive-sparing advantage.

items.title: Laser interstitial thermal therapy for medically intractable mesial temporal lobe epilepsy
items.citation: Kang JY, Wu C, Tracy J, et al. Epilepsia. 2016 Feb;57(2):325-34.
items.population: Adults with drug-resistant MTLE treated with MRI-guided LITT, 2011-2014
items.endpoints: Seizure freedom (Engel class), ablated mesial temporal volume, verbal memory
items.results: 60.5% of MTS patients free of disabling seizures vs 33.3% without MTS; ablation volume and verbal memory characterized, with variable memory change.
items.significance: Key early single-center series correlating ablation volume, neuropsych outcomes, and seizure freedom; reinforced MTS as a favorable predictor.
items.limitations: Small sample, single center, short follow-up, no open-surgery comparator.
items.clinicalBottomLine: LITT offers meaningful seizure freedom in MTLE, best in MTS patients, while documenting the ablation-volume/memory relationship.
items.whyMatters: LITT offers meaningful seizure freedom in MTLE, best in MTS patients, while documenting the ablation-volume/memory relationship.

items.title: Stereotactic laser amygdalohippocampotomy for mesial temporal lobe epilepsy
items.citation: Gross RE, Stern MA, Willie JT, et al. Ann Neurol. 2018 Mar;83(3):575-587.
items.studyDesign: Single-center prospective consecutive case series with >=12 months follow-up (Emory)
items.population: Consecutive adults with MTLE undergoing SLAH
items.endpoints: Seizure freedom (Engel I) at 1 year+; QOLIE-31; complications; verbal memory
items.results: At 1 year, 53.4% (95% CI 40.8-65.7) free of disabling seizures; 60.5% with MTS vs 33.3% without; QOLIE-31 improved significantly; verbal memory better than historical open-resection data.
items.significance: Largest single-center longitudinal SLAH series at publication; established durable seizure outcomes and favorable cognitive/QOL profile.
items.limitations: Single center, no concurrent open-surgery control, historical memory comparison.
items.clinicalBottomLine: SLAH yields ~53% one-year seizure freedom (higher with MTS) with low morbidity and favorable verbal memory versus open resection.
items.evidenceStrength: Single-center prospective consecutive case series with >=12 months follow-up (Emory)
items.whyMatters: SLAH yields ~53% one-year seizure freedom (higher with MTS) with low morbidity and favorable verbal memory versus open resection.

items.title: Stereotactic Laser Ablation for Mesial Temporal Lobe Epilepsy: a prospective, multicenter, single-arm study (SLATE)
items.citation: Sperling MR, Gross RE, Alvarez GE, McKhann GM, Salanova V, Gilmore J. Epilepsia. 2020 Jun;61(6):1183-1189.
items.studyDesign: Prospective, multicenter, single-arm interventional study (Visualase; NCT02844465)
items.population: Adults with drug-resistant MTLE due to mesial temporal sclerosis, multiple US sites
items.sampleSize: 167 enrolled; 114 treated and followed 12 months (21 sites)
items.endpoints: Primary: Engel Class I seizure freedom at 12 months; safety; quality of life
items.results: 12-month seizure-freedom rate 56.0% among 114 treated patients; QOL improvements; no SUDEP reported.
items.significance: Largest prospective multicenter trial of SLA/LITT for MTLE; higher-level efficacy/safety evidence for the Visualase system.
items.limitations: Single-arm (no randomized comparator), industry-sponsored, 12-month follow-up, MTS-restricted.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Multicenter prospective data confirm SLA achieves ~56% one-year seizure freedom in MTLE/MTS with a favorable safety profile.
items.evidenceStrength: Prospective, multicenter, single-arm interventional study (Visualase; NCT02844465)
items.whyMatters: Multicenter prospective data confirm SLA achieves ~56% one-year seizure freedom in MTLE/MTS with a favorable safety profile.

items.title: MR-guided stereotactic laser ablation of epileptogenic foci in children
items.citation: Curry DJ, Gowda A, McNichols RJ, Wilfong AA. Epilepsy Behav. 2012 Aug;24(4):408-414.
items.population: Pediatric drug-resistant epilepsy (Texas Children's), mixed foci including hypothalamic hamartoma and tuber
items.endpoints: Seizure freedom, procedural complications, short-term follow-up
items.results: All 5 patients seizure free with no surgical complications at 2-13 month follow-up; demonstrated feasibility of MRgLITT with real-time MR thermometry in children.
items.significance: Foundational paper launching the field of pediatric laser ablation for epilepsy.
items.limitations: Very small sample, short follow-up, heterogeneous pathology, single center, no comparator.
items.clinicalBottomLine: First demonstration that MRgLITT can safely and effectively ablate epileptogenic foci in children.
items.whyMatters: First demonstration that MRgLITT can safely and effectively ablate epileptogenic foci in children.

items.title: MR-guided laser ablation for the treatment of hypothalamic hamartomas
items.citation: Curry DJ, Raskin J, Ali I, Wilfong AA. Epilepsy Res. 2018 May;142:131-134.
items.population: Children/patients with hypothalamic hamartoma and refractory gelastic epilepsy (Texas Children's)
items.endpoints: Freedom from gelastic seizures at 1 year, repeat-ablation rate, complications
items.results: 93% free of gelastic seizures at 1 year; 23% required more than one ablation; one significant memory deficit and one worsening diabetes insipidus.
items.significance: Largest single-center HH laser ablation series of its era from the originating group; established SLA as a safe, effective primary surgical option for HH gelastic epilepsy.
items.limitations: Retrospective, single center, brief short-communication format, no long-term cognitive data, no comparator.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Stereotactic laser ablation is a safe, effective option for hypothalamic hamartoma, achieving ~93% gelastic seizure freedom at 1 year.
items.whyMatters: Stereotactic laser ablation is a safe, effective option for hypothalamic hamartoma, achieving ~93% gelastic seizure freedom at 1 year.

items.title: Stereotactic laser ablation for hypothalamic and deep intraventricular lesions
items.citation: Buckley RT, Wang AC, Miller JW, Novotny EJ, Ojemann JG. Neurosurg Focus. 2016 Oct;41(4):E10.
items.population: Patients (including children) with hypothalamic hamartomas and other deep/intraventricular lesions
items.endpoints: Procedural success, neurological/endocrine complications, hydrocephalus/shunt events
items.results: Ablation successful in all 12 with no permanent neurological/endocrine complications; 2 (17%) developed acute obstructive hydrocephalus or shunt malfunction.
items.significance: Early institutional experience characterizing safety of SLA for deep midline lesions including HH; documents obstructive-hydrocephalus risk.
items.limitations: Small heterogeneous cohort (not exclusively HH), single center, limited long-term seizure detail.
items.clinicalBottomLine: SLA for hypothalamic and deep intraventricular lesions is feasible without permanent neuro/endocrine deficits, though obstructive hydrocephalus (17%) is a notable risk.
items.whyMatters: SLA for hypothalamic and deep intraventricular lesions is feasible without permanent neuro/endocrine deficits, though obstructive hydrocephalus (17%) is a notable risk.

items.title: Single and staged laser interstitial thermal therapy ablation for cortical tubers causing refractory epilepsy in pediatric patients
items.citation: Tovar-Spinoza Z, Ziechmann R, Zyck S. Neurosurg Focus. 2018 Sep;45(3):E9.
items.studyDesign: Retrospective single-center case series of MR-guided LITT (single and staged) for cortical tubers
items.population: Pediatric patients with tuberous sclerosis complex and focal drug-resistant epilepsy from epileptogenic tubers
items.results: All 7 showed seizure improvement; >70% able to reduce antiseizure medications; single/staged tuber ablation feasible and minimally invasive.
items.significance: Supports MRgLITT as a minimally invasive option for tuber-related epilepsy in TSC, including staged ablation.
items.limitations: Very small, retrospective, single center, heterogeneous follow-up, no comparator.
items.clinicalBottomLine: MR-guided LITT of epileptogenic cortical tubers improves seizures and enables medication reduction in pediatric TSC.
items.evidenceStrength: Retrospective single-center case series of MR-guided LITT (single and staged) for cortical tubers
items.whyMatters: MR-guided LITT of epileptogenic cortical tubers improves seizures and enables medication reduction in pediatric TSC.

items.title: Corpus callosotomy via laser interstitial thermal therapy: a case series
items.citation: Caruso JP, Janjua MB, Dolce A, Price AV. J Neurosurg Pediatr. 2018 Dec;23(3):303-307.
items.population: Children with drug-refractory primary generalized epilepsy with drop (atonic) attacks
items.endpoints: Freedom from drop attacks; improvement in other seizures (Engel class); operative time; hospitalization; complications
items.results: Among 7 with >=3 months follow-up, 71% (5/7) free of drop attacks; 57% had improvement in other seizures; operative time 4-8 h; median stay 2 days; no complications.
items.significance: Early dedicated series supporting MRgLITT callosotomy as safe and effective for drop attacks.
items.limitations: Small sample, short/variable follow-up, retrospective, no control arm.
items.clinicalBottomLine: LITT callosotomy gives good drop-attack control with minimal morbidity and short stays in children with refractory generalized epilepsy.
items.whyMatters: LITT callosotomy gives good drop-attack control with minimal morbidity and short stays in children with refractory generalized epilepsy.

items.title: Corpus callosotomy performed with laser interstitial thermal therapy
items.citation: Roland JL, Akbari SHA, Salehi A, Smyth MD. J Neurosurg. 2021;134(1):314-322 (Epub 2019 Dec 13).
items.population: Patients with medically refractory epilepsy (including drop attacks) undergoing LITT anterior two-thirds callosotomy
items.results: Anterior two-thirds callosotomy via LITT in 10 patients; ~20% excellent and ~30% significant response in targeted seizure control (~50% meaningful responders).
items.significance: Reinforces LITT as a less invasive alternative to open callosotomy with meaningful drop-attack control.
items.limitations: Small retrospective series, no open-surgery comparator, heterogeneous extent of ablation.
items.clinicalBottomLine: LITT anterior two-thirds callosotomy is feasible and yields meaningful seizure control in about half of refractory patients.
items.whyMatters: LITT anterior two-thirds callosotomy is feasible and yields meaningful seizure control in about half of refractory patients.

items.title: Stereotactic laser anterior corpus callosotomy for Lennox-Gastaut syndrome
items.citation: Tao JX, Satzer D, Issa NP, et al. Epilepsia. 2020 Jun;61(6):1190-1200.
items.studyDesign: Retrospective single-institution case series (sequential cases Nov 2014-Jul 2019), 2-cannula SLACC technique
items.population: Patients with medically intractable Lennox-Gastaut syndrome undergoing stereotactic laser anterior corpus callosotomy
items.endpoints: Drop-attack/seizure outcomes vs open callosotomy benchmarks; safety; recovery
items.results: Seizure outcomes after SLACC comparable to previously reported open callosotomy outcomes, with reasonable safety, minimal postoperative discomfort, and short recovery.
items.significance: Suggests SLACC is an effective, less morbid alternative to open anterior callosotomy in LGS.
items.limitations: Retrospective, single center, historical (non-concurrent) comparison, sample size unverified.
items.clinicalBottomLine: SLACC offers open-callosotomy-comparable seizure control in LGS with less morbidity and faster recovery.
items.evidenceStrength: Retrospective single-institution case series (sequential cases Nov 2014-Jul 2019), 2-cannula SLACC technique
items.whyMatters: SLACC offers open-callosotomy-comparable seizure control in LGS with less morbidity and faster recovery.
items.changeNote: Verified: Tao et al., Epilepsia 2020;61(6):1190-1200; N=10, 8/10 with >80% drop-attack reduction.

items.title: Robot-assisted stereotactic laser ablation in medically intractable epilepsy: operative technique
items.citation: Gonzalez-Martinez J, Vadera S, Mullin J, et al. Oper Neurosurg (Neurosurgery). 2014 Jun;10(Suppl 2):167-172; discussion 172-173.
items.studyDesign: Technical/operative case series describing robot-assisted (ROSA) placement of laser ablation catheters
items.population: Patients with medically intractable focal/lesional epilepsy undergoing stereotactic laser ablation
items.sampleSize: Small operative-technique series (not powered for outcomes)
items.endpoints: Targeting accuracy, workflow/efficiency, feasibility and safety of robotic catheter placement
items.results: Robot-assisted LITT catheter placement reported accurate, efficient, minimally invasive, and safe; describes integrated ROSA + MRgLITT workflow.
items.significance: Foundational description of robotic stereotaxy (ROSA) for laser ablation catheter placement.
items.limitations: Technique paper, small numbers, no long-term seizure-outcome data, single center.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Robotic (ROSA) guidance enables accurate, efficient, minimally invasive placement of laser ablation catheters for intractable epilepsy.
items.evidenceStrength: Technical/operative case series describing robot-assisted (ROSA) placement of laser ablation catheters
items.whyMatters: Robotic (ROSA) guidance enables accurate, efficient, minimally invasive placement of laser ablation catheters for intractable epilepsy.
items.changeNote: Verified: Gonzalez-Martinez et al., Oper Neurosurg 2014;10 Suppl 2:167-172 (PMID 24448180).

items.title: Early surgical therapy for drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy: a randomized trial (ERSET)
items.citation: Engel J Jr, McDermott MP, Wiebe S, et al. JAMA. 2012 Mar 7;307(9):922-930.
items.studyDesign: Multicenter randomized controlled trial (16 US centers); stopped early for slow accrual
items.population: Patients >=12 years with MTLE and disabling seizures <=2 years after adequate trials of 2 ASMs
items.endpoints: Primary: seizure freedom during second year of follow-up; QOL; cognition
items.results: Seizure-free in year 2: 11/15 (73%) surgery vs 0/23 (0%) medical; QOL favored surgery; verbal memory decline more common with surgery.
items.significance: First RCT of early surgery in newly drug-resistant MTLE; showed surgery markedly superior to continued medical therapy, supporting earlier referral. Context for LITT comparators.
items.limitations: Small sample (terminated early), reducing power; short follow-up; memory-decline risk with dominant-side surgery.
items.clinicalBottomLine: In recently drug-resistant MTLE, early temporal resection achieves far higher seizure freedom than continued medication and argues against delaying surgical referral.
items.evidenceStrength: Multicenter randomized controlled trial (16 US centers); stopped early for slow accrual
items.whyMatters: In recently drug-resistant MTLE, early temporal resection achieves far higher seizure freedom than continued medication and argues against delaying surgical referral.

items.title: Long-term efficacy and safety of thalamic stimulation for drug-resistant partial epilepsy (SANTE 5-year)
items.citation: Salanova V, Witt T, Worth R, et al.; SANTE Study Group. Neurology. 2015 Mar 10;84(10):1017-1025.
items.studyDesign: 5-year long-term follow-up of the SANTE multicenter double-blind RCT of bilateral anterior thalamic nucleus DBS
items.population: Adults with drug-resistant partial (focal) onset seizures
items.endpoints: Median percent seizure reduction; >=50% responder rate; QOL; safety
items.results: Median seizure reduction 41% at 1 year and 69% at 5 years; responder rate 43% to 68%; 16% had >=6-month seizure-free periods; sustained QOL gains; no increased SUDEP/cognitive worsening overall.
items.significance: Established durable, increasing-over-time efficacy of ANT-DBS; supported regulatory approval for focal epilepsy. Shapes surgical algorithms for non-resective candidates.
items.limitations: Open-label after blinded phase; attrition; few seizure-free; device-related adverse events.
items.clinicalBottomLine: ANT-DBS provides durable, progressive seizure reduction (~69% median at 5 years) for drug-resistant focal epilepsy not amenable to resection/ablation.
items.evidenceStrength: 5-year long-term follow-up of the SANTE multicenter double-blind RCT of bilateral anterior thalamic nucleus DBS
items.whyMatters: ANT-DBS provides durable, progressive seizure reduction (~69% median at 5 years) for drug-resistant focal epilepsy not amenable to resection/ablation.

items.title: Nine-year prospective efficacy and safety of brain-responsive neurostimulation for focal epilepsy
items.citation: Nair DR, Laxer KD, Weber PB, et al. Neurology. 2020 Sep 1;95(9):e1244-e1256.
items.studyDesign: Prospective 9-year long-term open-label treatment study of the RNS System (closed-loop responsive neurostimulation)
items.population: Adults with medically intractable focal epilepsy from 1-2 foci, not candidates for or having failed resection
items.endpoints: Median percent seizure reduction; responder rate; seizure-free periods; QOL; safety/SUDEP
items.results: Median seizure reduction rose to ~75% at 9 years; 73% responder rate; 28% had >=6-month and 18% >=1-year seizure-free periods; QOL improved; reduced SUDEP vs expected.
items.significance: Longest prospective neurostimulation outcome data; confirms increasing RNS efficacy over time. Shapes surgical algorithms for eloquent/bilateral foci.
items.limitations: Open-label, no long-term sham control; attrition; palliative; specialized device expertise required.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Brain-responsive neurostimulation provides durable, increasing long-term benefit (~75% median reduction at 9 years) for drug-resistant focal epilepsy unsuitable for resection.
items.evidenceStrength: Prospective 9-year long-term open-label treatment study of the RNS System (closed-loop responsive neurostimulation)
items.whyMatters: Brain-responsive neurostimulation provides durable, increasing long-term benefit (~75% median reduction at 9 years) for drug-resistant focal epilepsy unsuitable for resection.

items.title: ROSA Brain stereotactic robot receives FDA 510(k) clearance (K151359, MedTech SA)
items.citation: FDA 510(k) database, K151359, MedTech S.A.; corroborated by ROSA Spine clearance press coverage
items.population: Patients undergoing functional/stereotactic neurosurgery (DBS, SEEG, biopsy)
items.endpoints: Substantial equivalence for stereotactic guidance/positioning of neurosurgical instruments
items.results: ROSA Brain cleared for robotic stereotactic assistance in cranial procedures; later acquired by Zimmer Biomet (2016) and built into the ROSA ONE platform. Exact decision day within 2015 not independently confirmed from primary letter.
items.significance: Establishes a credible robotic navigation/stereotaxy competitor that streamlines trajectory planning for SEEG, biopsy and DBS — workflows adjacent to and sometimes substituting for LITT referral pathways in epilepsy.
items.limitations: Primary FDA decision date not retrievable (mirror blocked); year-only date used.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Robotic stereotaxy gains an FDA foothold in cranial neurosurgery, intensifying platform competition around minimally invasive trajectory-based procedures.
items.whyMatters: Robotic stereotaxy gains an FDA foothold in cranial neurosurgery, intensifying platform competition around minimally invasive trajectory-based procedures.
items.changeNote: K151359 (ROSA Brain, MEDTECH S.A.) confirmed; exact FDA decision day not corroborable (FDA primary records proxy-blocked).

items.title: Renishaw neuromate frameless Gen II stereotactic robot cleared by FDA
items.citation: Renishaw press release; Medical Design & Outsourcing coverage of neuromate frameless Gen II FDA clearance
items.population: Functional neurosurgery patients (DBS electrode implantation, neuroendoscopy, biopsy)
items.endpoints: Substantial equivalence for robotic stereotactic positioning, frameless registration
items.results: Frameless Gen II version cleared October 2015; in August 2018 FDA cleared the neuromate Gen III with neuroinspire planning software enabling direct plan export to the robot.
items.significance: Adds another stereotactic robotics competitor (Renishaw) to the neurosurgical platform landscape; frameless workflow lowers a barrier to robot-assisted trajectory procedures.
items.limitations: Year-month date approximate; primary FDA letter not directly retrieved.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Robotics competition broadens beyond ROSA, pressuring incumbents on planning-to-execution workflow integration.
items.whyMatters: Robotics competition broadens beyond ROSA, pressuring incumbents on planning-to-execution workflow integration.
items.changeNote: Verified: neuromate frameless Gen II (K132755) clearance announced 2015-10-16.

items.title: icometrix icobrain receives first FDA 510(k) clearance for quantitative brain MRI
items.citation: FDA 510(k) K161148; icometrix press release 'FDA clears icometrix image quantification software'
items.population: Patients undergoing brain MRI for neurological disorders
items.endpoints: Substantial equivalence for automated brain volumetry / longitudinal change quantification (product code LLZ)
items.results: K161148 cleared ~August 2016 — icobrain's first US clearance for AI quantification of clinical brain MRI; followed by K181939 (Nov 2018, CT) and K192130 (Dec 2019).
items.significance: Entry of AI quantitative neuro-imaging into US practice; supports tumor/lesion characterization and surgical decision-making that feeds the referral funnel for ablation and resection.
items.limitations: Exact decision date corroborated via database snippet, not primary letter.
items.clinicalBottomLine: AI neuro-imaging begins clearing FDA, strengthening the diagnostic pipeline that identifies LITT-eligible lesions.
items.whyMatters: AI neuro-imaging begins clearing FDA, strengthening the diagnostic pipeline that identifies LITT-eligible lesions.

items.title: CorTechs Labs NeuroQuant cleared with expanded features and LesionQuant module (K170981)
items.citation: FDA 510(k) K170981; ITN Online 'CorTechs Labs Receives FDA 510(k) Clearance for NeuroQuant'
items.population: Patients undergoing volumetric brain MRI; normative reference ages 3-100
items.endpoints: Substantial equivalence for automated brain segmentation/quantification, longitudinal tracking, FLAIR lesion quantification (LesionQuant)
items.results: K170981 cleared September 2017, adding longitudinal tracking and the LesionQuant module to NeuroQuant (original clearance K061855, 2006).
items.significance: Strengthens quantitative neuro-imaging that supports lesion detection, treatment-response assessment and surgical planning — diagnostic infrastructure upstream of ablation referral.
items.limitations: Exact decision day partially verified (month-level).
items.clinicalBottomLine: Quantitative MRI tooling matures, supporting the imaging pipeline that funnels patients toward minimally invasive neurosurgical options.
items.whyMatters: Quantitative MRI tooling matures, supporting the imaging pipeline that funnels patients toward minimally invasive neurosurgical options.

items.title: Brainlab Elements Cranial SRS & Spine SRS radiosurgery planning software cleared by FDA (K170816)
items.citation: Brainlab press release (Business Wire, 2017-09-21); FDANews coverage of Elements Spine/Cranial SRS clearance
items.population: Patients undergoing stereotactic radiosurgery (AVM, pituitary adenoma, vestibular schwannoma, glioma, meningioma, large brain metastases)
items.endpoints: Substantial equivalence for indication-specific radiosurgery treatment planning
items.results: Cleared September 2017; enables radiosurgery plans in under 15 minutes for multiple cranial indications. Brainlab also won FDA clearance (Dec 2017) for Contrast Clearance Analysis to assess post-treatment tumor characteristics.
items.significance: Bolsters SRS — the principal non-surgical competitor to LITT for brain metastases and radiation necrosis — improving planning efficiency and competitive positioning of radiosurgery vs ablation.
items.limitations: K-number reported via secondary sources; exact device list not confirmed from primary letter.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Streamlined SRS planning strengthens radiosurgery as the chief alternative to LITT for metastases and necrosis.
items.whyMatters: Streamlined SRS planning strengthens radiosurgery as the chief alternative to LITT for metastases and necrosis.
items.changeNote: Verified: Brainlab Elements Cranial/Spine SRS (K170816) announced 2017-09-21.

items.title: FDA Class I recall of Monteris NeuroBlate laser probes/system for unexpected probe heating
items.citation: FDA Class I recall; Medscape 'NeuroBlate Probe May Overheat, FDA Class I Recall Warns'; MassDevice and MPO coverage
items.endpoints: Risk of unintended probe-tip heating, brain tissue damage, and CO2 cooling-gas leakage during MRI
items.results: Monteris initiated a voluntary recall on Oct 5, 2017; FDA designated it Class I (most serious) in March 2018 following instances of unintended heating/probe damage and MR scan restrictions.
items.significance: Major safety/quality event for the No. 2 LITT platform; created MR-scan use restrictions, raised liability concerns, and opened a competitive window for Visualase before Monteris remediated.
items.limitations: Recall scope limited to specified probe lots/configurations.
items.clinicalBottomLine: A Class I recall on NeuroBlate temporarily dented confidence in the LITT category and Monteris specifically.
items.whyMatters: A Class I recall on NeuroBlate temporarily dented confidence in the LITT category and Monteris specifically.

items.title: Monteris receives FDA clearance for NeuroBlate Optic laser probe with fiber-optic controlled cooling
items.citation: Monteris Medical press release (PR Newswire); NeuroNews International coverage of NeuroBlate Optic clearance
items.endpoints: Substantial equivalence for laser probe with fiber-optic (vs metal thermocouple) cooling control
items.results: Cleared October 2018; replacing the metal thermocouple with a fiber-optic element resolved the MR-heating failure mode behind the Class I recall and allowed Monteris to lift MR scan restrictions.
items.significance: Direct remediation of the recall; restores NeuroBlate competitiveness against Visualase and removes a major utilization barrier.
items.limitations: Exact decision day month-level.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Monteris recovers from its recall by re-engineering the probe, re-leveling the LITT competitive field.
items.whyMatters: Monteris recovers from its recall by re-engineering the probe, re-leveling the LITT competitive field.

items.title: Zimmer Biomet ROSA ONE Brain application cleared by FDA (K182417)
items.citation: FDA 510(k) K182417, ROSA ONE Brain Application; FDA/Innolitics submission record
items.population: Cranial stereotactic neurosurgery patients (SEEG, DBS, biopsy)
items.endpoints: Substantial equivalence for robotic stereotactic guidance in cranial procedures
items.results: ROSA ONE Brain (K182417) cleared in 2018, consolidating brain robotics under the unified Zimmer Biomet ROSA ONE platform; in 2019 Zimmer received clearance for a combined brain/spine/knee ROSA system.
items.significance: Strengthens the leading neuro robotics competitor with a modern platform that accelerates SEEG/DBS/biopsy and trajectory-based workflows adjacent to LITT.
items.limitations: Year-only date used; exact decision day not confirmed from primary letter.
items.clinicalBottomLine: ROSA ONE Brain entrenches robotic stereotaxy as a competitive workflow standard in functional/epilepsy neurosurgery.
items.whyMatters: ROSA ONE Brain entrenches robotic stereotaxy as a competitive workflow standard in functional/epilepsy neurosurgery.
items.changeNote: Verified: ROSA ONE Brain (K182417) FDA decision 2019-02-07.

items.title: icometrix icobrain ep cleared by FDA for AI imaging analysis in epilepsy (K192962)
items.citation: FDA 510(k) K192962; icometrix press release 'icometrix expands icobrain portfolio with AI image analysis solution for epilepsy'
items.population: Patients with intractable/drug-resistant epilepsy undergoing brain MRI
items.endpoints: Substantial equivalence for AI detection of subtle cortical abnormalities and hippocampal asymmetry
items.results: K192962 cleared Feb 2020 (announced Feb 10, 2020); icobrain ep aids identification of epileptogenic substrate including hippocampal sclerosis.
items.significance: AI-assisted localization of epileptogenic lesions expands the pool of surgically/ablatively treatable epilepsy patients — directly supporting LITT (e.g., SLAH) referral.
items.limitations: Adjunctive; does not by itself change procedure choice.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Better AI lesion localization in epilepsy enlarges the candidate pool for minimally invasive ablation.
items.whyMatters: Better AI lesion localization in epilepsy enlarges the candidate pool for minimally invasive ablation.

items.title: AMA CPT Editorial Panel establishes dedicated Category I LITT codes 61736/61737 (effective 2022) — corrects assumed 2016 date
items.citation: Monteris Medical announcement of new AMA Category I CPT codes; AGS Health 2022 CPT code update; AANS/CNS code application
items.endpoints: Establishment of Category I CPT codes for intracranial LITT with MRI guidance
items.results: CPT Editorial Panel approved 61736 (single trajectory, one simple lesion) and 61737 (multiple trajectories/complex lesions) at its Oct 1-2, 2020 meeting, EFFECTIVE Jan 1, 2022. IMPORTANT CORRECTION: these codes did NOT exist in 2016 — before 2022, intracranial LITT was billed via unlisted/adjacent codes. No LITT-specific CMS NCD/LCD existed during 2015-2020.
items.significance: Dedicated Category I codes (panel action 2020) were the single biggest reimbursement catalyst for LITT, replacing unlisted-code uncertainty and enabling consistent payment — a structural utilization tailwind.
items.limitations: Codes effective 2022 (just outside window); dated milestone is the Oct 2020 panel action. CMS/payer in-window revision dates unverified (pages blocked).
items.clinicalBottomLine: The path to dedicated LITT reimbursement crystallized with the Oct 2020 AMA panel action — not 2016 as commonly assumed.
items.whyMatters: The path to dedicated LITT reimbursement crystallized with the Oct 2020 AMA panel action — not 2016 as commonly assumed.

items.title: Maintenance Therapy With Tumor-Treating Fields Plus Temozolomide vs Temozolomide Alone for Glioblastoma: A Randomized Clinical Trial (EF-14 interim)
items.citation: Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner AA, et al. JAMA. 2015;314(23):2535-2543.
items.studyDesign: Phase 3 randomized open-label trial, prespecified interim analysis (first 315 patients)
items.population: Newly diagnosed glioblastoma after maximal resection and concurrent chemoradiation
items.significance: Interim crossing of the efficacy boundary halted enrollment and supported the October 2015 FDA approval of TTFields (Optune) for newly diagnosed GBM.
items.limitations: Interim/immature OS data; open-label, no sham-device control.
items.clinicalBottomLine: First randomized signal that adding TTFields to maintenance temozolomide improves PFS and OS in newly diagnosed GBM; superseded by the 2017 final analysis but historically practice-changing.
items.evidenceStrength: Phase 3 randomized open-label trial, prespecified interim analysis (first 315 patients)
items.whyMatters: First randomized signal that adding TTFields to maintenance temozolomide improves PFS and OS in newly diagnosed GBM; superseded by the 2017 final analysis but historically practice-changing.

items.title: Effect of Tumor-Treating Fields Plus Maintenance Temozolomide vs Maintenance Temozolomide Alone on Survival in Patients With Glioblastoma: A Randomized Clinical Trial (EF-14 final)
items.citation: Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner A, et al. JAMA. 2017;318(23):2306-2316.
items.studyDesign: Phase 3 randomized open-label trial (2:1), final full-dataset analysis, multicenter
items.population: Newly diagnosed glioblastoma after resection/biopsy and radiochemotherapy
items.significance: Only therapy since temozolomide to improve OS in newly diagnosed GBM; basis for NCCN category 1 listing and broad FDA/guideline adoption of Optune.
items.limitations: Open-label, no sham device; efficacy depends on device wear-time/compliance; control arm not blinded.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Adding TTFields to maintenance temozolomide significantly prolongs OS and PFS in newly diagnosed GBM and is a standard-of-care option.
items.evidenceStrength: Phase 3 randomized open-label trial (2:1), final full-dataset analysis, multicenter
items.whyMatters: Adding TTFields to maintenance temozolomide significantly prolongs OS and PFS in newly diagnosed GBM and is a standard-of-care option.

items.title: Lomustine-temozolomide combination therapy versus standard temozolomide therapy in patients with newly diagnosed glioblastoma with methylated MGMT promoter (CeTeG/NOA-09)
items.citation: Herrlinger U, Tzaridis T, Mack F, et al. Lancet. 2019;393(10172):678-688.
items.studyDesign: Phase 3, randomized, open-label trial; 17 German university hospitals
items.population: Newly diagnosed GBM with MGMT promoter methylation, age 18-70, KPS >=70
items.results: Median OS 48.1 mo (lomustine-TMZ) vs 31.4 mo (TMZ) (HR 0.60; 95% CI 0.35-1.03; P=0.0492 log-rank). Grade >=3 AEs 59% vs 51%; no treatment-related deaths.
items.significance: Largest OS gain reported in an MGMT-methylated GBM RCT; influential option in fit, methylated patients (notably in European practice).
items.limitations: Small (n=141); borderline P value; OS primary in a small trial; open-label; higher hematologic toxicity and lower treatment completion with the combination.
items.clinicalBottomLine: In fit patients with MGMT-methylated newly diagnosed GBM, lomustine+temozolomide may substantially extend survival vs temozolomide alone, with caution given trial size.
items.evidenceStrength: Phase 3, randomized, open-label trial; 17 German university hospitals
items.whyMatters: In fit patients with MGMT-methylated newly diagnosed GBM, lomustine+temozolomide may substantially extend survival vs temozolomide alone, with caution given trial size.

items.title: Effect of Nivolumab vs Bevacizumab in Patients With Recurrent Glioblastoma: The CheckMate 143 Phase 3 Randomized Clinical Trial
items.citation: Reardon DA, Brandes AA, Omuro A, et al. JAMA Oncol. 2020;6(7):1003-1010.
items.population: First-recurrence glioblastoma after radiotherapy plus temozolomide
items.results: Median OS 9.8 mo (nivolumab) vs 10.0 mo (bevacizumab) (HR 1.04; 95% CI 0.83-1.30; P=0.76). 12-mo OS 42% both arms. ORR 7.8% vs 23.1%.
items.significance: Definitive negative trial establishing that single-agent PD-1 blockade does not improve OS in recurrent GBM.
items.limitations: Bevacizumab comparator itself lacks proven OS benefit; unselected population; open-label.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Nivolumab monotherapy is not effective in recurrent GBM; exploratory hints in methylated-MGMT, steroid-free patients remain hypothesis-generating.
items.whyMatters: Nivolumab monotherapy is not effective in recurrent GBM; exploratory hints in methylated-MGMT, steroid-free patients remain hypothesis-generating.

items.title: Radiation plus Procarbazine, CCNU, and Vincristine in Low-Grade Glioma (RTOG 9802 long-term)
items.citation: Buckner JC, Shaw EG, Pugh SL, et al. N Engl J Med. 2016;374(14):1344-1355.
items.studyDesign: Phase 3 randomized controlled trial, mature/long-term follow-up (NCT00003375)
items.population: High-risk WHO grade 2 glioma (age <40 with subtotal resection/biopsy, or age >=40 any extent); astrocytoma, oligodendroglioma, oligoastrocytoma
items.results: Median OS 13.3 yr (RT+PCV) vs 7.8 yr (RT alone); HR death 0.59, P=0.003. Median PFS 10.4 vs 4.0 yr; HR 0.50, P<0.001. 10-yr OS 60% vs 40%. Later genomic analyses showed benefit concentrated in IDH-mutant tumors.
items.significance: First RCT to show a definitive OS benefit from adding chemotherapy to RT in low-grade glioma; established RT+PCV as standard of care for high-risk LGG.
items.limitations: Pre-molecular-era enrollment (1998-2002); molecular subtyping retrospective; PCV tolerability concerns.
items.clinicalBottomLine: In high-risk LGG, RT followed by adjuvant PCV nearly doubles median OS vs RT alone, a practice-defining standard of care.
items.evidenceStrength: Phase 3 randomized controlled trial, mature/long-term follow-up (NCT00003375)
items.whyMatters: In high-risk LGG, RT followed by adjuvant PCV nearly doubles median OS vs RT alone, a practice-defining standard of care.

items.title: Temozolomide chemotherapy versus radiotherapy in high-risk low-grade glioma (EORTC 22033-26033): a randomised, open-label, phase 3 intergroup study
items.citation: Baumert BG, Hegi ME, van den Bent MJ, et al. Lancet Oncol. 2016;17(11):1521-1532.
items.studyDesign: Phase 3, randomized, open-label intergroup study (78 centres, 19 countries)
items.population: High-risk WHO grade 2 glioma (>=1 high-risk feature)
items.results: No significant overall PFS difference: HR 1.16 (95% CI 0.9-1.5), P=0.22. In IDH-mutant/non-codeleted tumors, PFS was longer with RT than TMZ (HR 1.86, 95% CI 1.21-2.87, P=0.0043).
items.significance: First-line single-modality TMZ is not superior to RT; molecular subtype predicts differential benefit, reinforcing upfront RT in astrocytic LGG.
items.limitations: PFS (not OS) primary; single-modality comparison; molecular subgroup analyses exploratory.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Substituting upfront TMZ for RT does not improve outcomes in high-risk LGG; IDH-mutant/non-codeleted astrocytomas may do worse with TMZ alone, favoring an RT-based strategy.
items.evidenceStrength: Phase 3, randomized, open-label intergroup study (78 centres, 19 countries)
items.whyMatters: Substituting upfront TMZ for RT does not improve outcomes in high-risk LGG; IDH-mutant/non-codeleted astrocytomas may do worse with TMZ alone, favoring an RT-based strategy.

items.title: Interim results from the CATNON trial (EORTC 26053-22054): concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide for 1p/19q non-co-deleted anaplastic glioma, a phase 3 randomised intergroup study
items.citation: van den Bent MJ, Baumert B, Erridge SC, et al. Lancet. 2017;390(10103):1645-1653.
items.studyDesign: Phase 3, 2x2 factorial, randomized, open-label intergroup study (NCT00626990)
items.population: Newly diagnosed 1p/19q non-co-deleted anaplastic glioma (WHO grade III)
items.results: Adjuvant TMZ: 5-yr OS 55.9% vs 44.1%; HR death 0.65 (99.145% CI 0.45-0.93); median PFS 42.8 vs 19.0 mo. Subsequent 2021 analysis (Lancet Oncol; DOI 10.1016/S1470-2045(21)00090-5) showed adjuvant TMZ benefit confined to IDH-mutant tumors.
items.significance: Established a role for adjuvant temozolomide after RT in non-codeleted (largely IDH-mutant astrocytic) anaplastic glioma.
items.limitations: Interim analysis; concurrent-TMZ arm and molecular data immature in 2017; benefit later shown to be IDH-driven.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Adjuvant TMZ after RT improves survival in 1p/19q non-codeleted anaplastic glioma; benefit is in IDH-mutant disease, while IDH-wildtype should be treated as glioblastoma.
items.evidenceStrength: Phase 3, 2x2 factorial, randomized, open-label intergroup study (NCT00626990)
items.whyMatters: Adjuvant TMZ after RT improves survival in 1p/19q non-codeleted anaplastic glioma; benefit is in IDH-mutant disease, while IDH-wildtype should be treated as glioblastoma.

items.title: Early results from the CODEL trial for anaplastic oligodendrogliomas: is temozolomide futile?
items.citation: Jaeckle KA, Ballman KV, van den Bent M, et al. Neuro-Oncology. 2021;23(3):457-467.
items.population: Newly diagnosed 1p/19q co-deleted anaplastic glioma (original design)
items.sampleSize: 36 (12 per arm: RT alone, RT+TMZ, TMZ alone; original design)
items.results: At median 7.5 yr, TMZ-alone strongly inferior: 83.3% (10/12) progressed vs 37.5% (9/24) on RT-containing arms. Median PFS 2.9 yr (TMZ alone) vs not reached; HR 3.12 (95% CI 1.26-7.69), P<0.01.
items.significance: TMZ monotherapy is inadequate for codeleted anaplastic glioma; led to redesign comparing RT+PCV vs RT+TMZ and supports RT-based combined modality.
items.limitations: Very small original-design cohort (n=36); underpowered; definitive redesigned comparison still maturing.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Radiotherapy must remain part of upfront treatment for 1p/19q codeleted anaplastic glioma; TMZ alone is inferior.
items.whyMatters: Radiotherapy must remain part of upfront treatment for 1p/19q codeleted anaplastic glioma; TMZ alone is inferior.

items.title: Effect of Radiosurgery Alone vs Radiosurgery With Whole Brain Radiation Therapy on Cognitive Function in Patients With 1 to 3 Brain Metastases (NCCTG N0574/Alliance)
items.citation: Brown PD, Jaeckle K, Ballman KV, et al. JAMA. 2016;316(4):401-409.
items.population: Adults with 1-3 brain metastases, each <3 cm, eligible for SRS
items.endpoints: Cognitive deterioration at 3 months (primary); OS, intracranial control, QoL (secondary)
items.results: Cognitive deterioration at 3 mo 91.7% (SRS+WBRT) vs 63.5% (SRS alone), P<0.001. Intracranial control better with WBRT, but no OS benefit (median OS 10.4 mo SRS alone vs 7.4 mo SRS+WBRT). QoL better with SRS alone.
items.significance: Landmark trial establishing that adding WBRT to SRS worsens cognition without OS benefit; major driver of SRS-alone as standard for limited brain mets.
items.limitations: Underpowered for OS; higher intracranial failure with SRS alone requires MRI surveillance and salvage.
items.clinicalBottomLine: For 1-3 brain metastases amenable to SRS, use SRS alone (not routine upfront WBRT) to preserve cognition and QoL, with MRI surveillance.
items.whyMatters: For 1-3 brain metastases amenable to SRS, use SRS alone (not routine upfront WBRT) to preserve cognition and QoL, with MRI surveillance.

items.title: Postoperative stereotactic radiosurgery compared with whole brain radiotherapy for resected metastatic brain disease (NCCTG N107C/CEC.3): a multicentre, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trial
items.citation: Brown PD, Ballman KV, Cerhan JH, et al. Lancet Oncol. 2017;18(8):1049-1060.
items.studyDesign: Multicenter (48 US/Canada sites), randomized, phase 3 trial
items.population: Adults with 1 resected brain metastasis, resection cavity <5.0 cm
items.endpoints: Co-primary: cognitive-deterioration-free survival and overall survival
items.results: Cognitive-deterioration-free survival longer with SRS (median 3.7 vs 3.0 mo; HR 0.47; P<0.0001). No OS difference (median 12.2 vs 11.6 mo; HR 1.07; 95% CI 0.76-1.50; P=0.70). Surgical-bed/intracranial control better with WBRT.
items.significance: Practice-changing trial establishing cavity SRS (not WBRT) as preferred postoperative adjuvant therapy after brain-met resection.
items.limitations: Higher local/intracranial recurrence with SRS requires surveillance/salvage; single-fraction cavity SRS only.
items.clinicalBottomLine: After resection of a brain metastasis, postoperative cavity SRS is preferred over WBRT, with equivalent survival and better cognitive preservation.
items.evidenceStrength: Multicenter (48 US/Canada sites), randomized, phase 3 trial
items.whyMatters: After resection of a brain metastasis, postoperative cavity SRS is preferred over WBRT, with equivalent survival and better cognitive preservation.

items.title: Dexamethasone and supportive care with or without whole brain radiotherapy in NSCLC brain metastases unsuitable for resection or stereotactic radiotherapy (QUARTZ): a phase 3 non-inferiority randomised trial
items.citation: Mulvenna P, Nankivell M, Barton R, et al. Lancet. 2016;388(10055):2004-2014.
items.population: NSCLC with brain metastases unsuitable for surgery or SRS
items.endpoints: Quality-adjusted life-years (primary); OS, QoL, steroid use, adverse events (secondary)
items.results: No OS difference (median 9.2 vs 8.5 wk; HR 1.06; 95% CI 0.90-1.26). QALY difference only 4.7 days (within non-inferiority margin). No meaningful difference in QoL or dexamethasone use.
items.significance: Demonstrated WBRT provides negligible clinical benefit for poor-prognosis NSCLC brain mets, supporting supportive-care-only in this population.
items.limitations: Possible benefit in younger (<60 yr), better-prognosis patients; specific to NSCLC unsuitable for surgery/SRS.
items.clinicalBottomLine: For NSCLC patients with brain mets unsuitable for surgery/SRS and poor prognosis, optimal supportive care alone is reasonable; WBRT adds little to survival or QoL.
items.whyMatters: For NSCLC patients with brain mets unsuitable for surgery/SRS and poor prognosis, optimal supportive care alone is reasonable; WBRT adds little to survival or QoL.

items.title: Hippocampal Avoidance During Whole-Brain Radiotherapy Plus Memantine for Patients With Brain Metastases: Phase III Trial NRG Oncology CC001
items.citation: Brown PD, Gondi V, Pugh S, et al. J Clin Oncol. 2020;38(10):1019-1029.
items.population: Adults with brain metastases (no mets within 5 mm of hippocampus) planned for WBRT
items.endpoints: Time to cognitive failure (primary); patient-reported symptoms, intracranial PFS, OS, toxicity (secondary)
items.results: HA-WBRT+memantine lowered cognitive-failure risk (adjusted HR 0.74; 95% CI 0.58-0.95; P=0.02), with less deterioration in executive function and memory and fewer patient-reported symptoms. No difference in intracranial PFS or OS.
items.significance: Established hippocampal-avoidance WBRT plus memantine as a standard-of-care option when WBRT is needed in patients without perihippocampal disease.
items.limitations: Excludes perihippocampal mets; applies to a shrinking WBRT-selected population in the SRS era.
items.clinicalBottomLine: When WBRT is indicated, use hippocampal-avoidance technique plus memantine to preserve cognition and reduce symptoms without compromising tumor control or survival.
items.whyMatters: When WBRT is indicated, use hippocampal-avoidance technique plus memantine to preserve cognition and reduce symptoms without compromising tumor control or survival.

items.title: Intermediate-risk meningioma: initial outcomes from NRG Oncology RTOG 0539
items.citation: Rogers CL, Won M, Vogelbaum MA, et al. J Neurosurg. 2018;129(1):35-47.
items.studyDesign: Prospective, single-arm, risk-adapted phase II cooperative-group trial
items.population: Newly diagnosed WHO grade II (atypical) meningioma after gross total resection (Simpson I-III), or recurrent WHO grade I (any resection)
items.endpoints: 3-year PFS (primary) vs historical control; OS, local failure, toxicity (secondary)
items.results: 3-yr PFS 93.8% (exceeded historical ~70%); 3-yr OS 96%; 3-yr local failure 4.1%. Adjuvant RT (54 Gy/30 fx) well tolerated; no grade >=4 toxicity.
items.significance: First prospective cooperative-group evidence supporting adjuvant RT after GTR of atypical meningioma; helped establish 54 Gy for intermediate-risk disease.
items.limitations: Single-arm (no randomized observation comparator); modest N; relatively short follow-up.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Adjuvant fractionated RT (54 Gy) yields excellent 3-yr PFS in intermediate-risk meningioma and supports its use after GTR of atypical meningioma.
items.evidenceStrength: Prospective, single-arm, risk-adapted phase II cooperative-group trial
items.whyMatters: Adjuvant fractionated RT (54 Gy) yields excellent 3-yr PFS in intermediate-risk meningioma and supports its use after GTR of atypical meningioma.

items.title: High-risk meningioma: initial outcomes from NRG Oncology/RTOG 0539
items.citation: Rogers CL, Won M, Vogelbaum MA, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys. 2020;106(4):790-799.
items.venue: International Journal of Radiation Oncology Biology Physics
items.studyDesign: Prospective, single-arm phase II cooperative-group trial
items.population: New/recurrent WHO grade III any resection; recurrent grade II any resection; new grade II after subtotal resection
items.results: 3-yr PFS 58.8%; 3-yr local control 68.9%; 3-yr OS 78.6%. Treatment 60 Gy IMRT with simultaneous integrated boost; higher-than-expected adverse events including some grade 3+.
items.significance: Prospective benchmark for high-risk meningioma treated with dose-escalated RT (60 Gy); informs current management and trial design (e.g., NRG-BN003).
items.limitations: Single-arm, small N, heterogeneous high-risk population; no randomization.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Even with 60 Gy IMRT, high-risk meningioma recurs frequently (~59% 3-yr PFS), a benchmark underscoring the need for intensified/systemic therapy.
items.evidenceStrength: Prospective, single-arm phase II cooperative-group trial
items.whyMatters: Even with 60 Gy IMRT, high-risk meningioma recurs frequently (~59% 3-yr PFS), a benchmark underscoring the need for intensified/systemic therapy.

items.title: EANO guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of meningiomas
items.citation: Goldbrunner R, Minniti G, Preusser M, et al. Lancet Oncol. 2016;17(9):e383-e391.
items.studyDesign: Evidence-based consensus clinical practice guideline (EANO task force)
items.results: Established European standards: observation for small asymptomatic meningiomas; surgery as primary therapy; RT for grade III and incompletely resected/recurrent grade II; stereotactic radiosurgery for small/surgically inaccessible lesions.
items.significance: First comprehensive EANO meningioma guideline; widely used risk-stratified management framework (later updated in 2021, DOI 10.1093/neuonc/noab150).
items.limitations: Pre-2021 WHO/molecular era; partly superseded by the 2021 EANO update integrating molecular markers.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Defined the European standard-of-care algorithm (observe vs resect vs irradiate) for meningioma by grade and resection status.
items.evidenceStrength: Evidence-based consensus clinical practice guideline (EANO task force)
items.whyMatters: Defined the European standard-of-care algorithm (observe vs resect vs irradiate) for meningioma by grade and resection status.

items.title: The ROAM/EORTC-1308 trial: Radiation versus Observation following surgical resection of Atypical Meningioma: study protocol for a randomised controlled trial
items.citation: Jenkinson MD, Javadpour M, Haylock BJ, et al. Trials. 2015;16:519.
items.studyDesign: International multicentre phase III randomized controlled trial (1:1) - study protocol (primary results pending, expected 2025-2026)
items.population: Adults after gross total resection of intracranial atypical (WHO grade II) meningioma
items.endpoints: Time to MRI evidence of recurrence (PFS) (primary); toxicity, QoL, neurocognition, time to second-line, OS, cost/QALY (secondary)
items.results: Not yet published; trial accrued/closed with primary PFS readout expected 2025-2026. Assumes 40% (observation) vs 20% (RT) recurrence; intervention 60 Gy/30 fx vs active MRI surveillance.
items.significance: First and definitive randomized trial of adjuvant RT vs observation after GTR of atypical meningioma; with NRG-BN003 (NCT03180268) will provide Level I evidence for this common clinical dilemma.
items.limitations: Efficacy results pending; only protocol and RT-QA substudy data available to date.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Practice-defining meningioma RCT in progress; until it reports, adjuvant RT after GTR of atypical meningioma remains individualized. Flag for re-check in 2026.
items.evidenceStrength: International multicentre phase III randomized controlled trial (1:1) - study protocol (primary results pending, expected 2025-2026)
items.whyMatters: Practice-defining meningioma RCT in progress; until it reports, adjuvant RT after GTR of atypical meningioma remains individualized. Flag for re-check in 2026.
items.changeNote: Protocol verified (Trials 2015;16:519). Primary PFS results NOT yet published as of 2026-06; endpoint slated to report 2026 - re-check.

items.title: ClearPoint Neuro announces investment of up to ~$105M by Oberland Capital, with $30M funded at closing
items.citation: ClearPoint Neuro, Inc. (CLPT) press release / SEC Form 8-K. ClearPoint Neuro Announces Investment by Oberland Capital. May 12, 2025.
items.venue: ClearPoint Neuro IR / SEC EDGAR (company PR + filing)
items.results: Note financing with Oberland Capital of up to ~$105M (initial $30M at closing, plus optional tranches of $25M and $50M through Dec 31, 2026) and a registered direct stock purchase (~275,808 shares at $12.69). Note: headline framing varies between ~$105M and ~$110M across outlets; SEC 8-K is authoritative.
items.significance: Non-dilutive growth capital extends the runway for a key LITT navigation/delivery enabler (ClearPoint PRISM platform and SmartFrame stereotactic system), supporting capacity to expand the neuro-ablation install base and pharma/cell-therapy delivery partnerships.
items.limitations: Press-release framing; exact committed vs. optional amounts differ across re-published versions — confirm against the 8-K.
items.clinicalBottomLine: Non-dilutive growth capital extends the runway for a key LITT navigation/delivery enabler (ClearPoint PRISM platform and SmartFrame stereotactic system), supporting capacity to expand the neuro-ablation install base and pharma/cell-therapy delivery partnerships.
items.whyMatters: Non-dilutive growth capital extends the runway for a key LITT navigation/delivery enabler (ClearPoint PRISM platform and SmartFrame stereotactic system), supporting capacity to expand the neuro-ablation install base and pharma/cell-therapy delivery partnerships.

items.title: Insightec raises $150M to expand incisionless (focused ultrasound) neurosurgery
items.citation: Insightec press release (PR Newswire); covered by MedTech Dive, MassDevice. INSIGHTEC ANNOUNCES $150M FINANCING TO FUND CONTINUED GROWTH. June 18, 2024.
items.venue: PR Newswire / MedTech Dive (company PR + trade)
items.results: $150M equity financing led by Fidelity, co-led by Nexus Neurotech Ventures and Ally Bridge Group; outlets report ~$1.3B post-money valuation. Funds incisionless MR-guided focused ultrasound platform (Exablate Neuro).
